summary:
Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder characterized by antibody attack on postsynaptic components at the neuromuscular junction, leading to disrupted acetylcholine signaling and resulting muscle weakness. This pathology occurs exclusively within the neuromuscular junction, making it the definitive site of dysfunction despite broader anatomical references elsewhere.

The core issue in myasthenia gravis is immune-mediated damage to acetylcholine receptors or MuSK proteins at the neuromuscular junction, which impairs neurotransmission and causes fluctuating muscle fatigue. All available evidence confirms this specific mechanism defines its pathophysiology. 

Myasthenia gravis involves targeted destruction of presynaptic or postsynaptic molecules critical for synaptic communication across the neuromuscular junction through autoreactive antibodies against acetylcholine receptor or mu-sk protein complexes located precisely where motor neurons connect to skeletal muscles; thus, any treatment strategy aims either to block degradation processes like cholinesterase activity using pyridostigmine or suppress immunological responses such as corticosteroids/IVIG therapy—all focused around restoring normal neuromuscular transmission dynamics rooted firmly in structural integrity of synapses themselves.

Only one correct choice exists based upon current medical understanding regarding precise localization of underlying defect—and that being strictly confined within the neuromuscular junction structure
